articles.title: The great digital fatigue: How digital burnout is changing social media use
articles.summary: Users are pulling back from social media, with 55% posting less and 53% limiting audiences, while 47% have deleted apps due to stress. Over half say maintaining a presence feels like work, especially Gen Z, and 44% cite political content as a reason to disengage. Stepping away brings mixed emotions, with 27% feeling peaceful but 22% anxious, highlighting how platforms now feel more like obligations than connection.

articles.title: The Fortune 500 is Deleting the CMO Title
articles.summary: The CMO title is disappearing, with only 36% of Fortune 500 companies still using it, down from 49% last year. Companies are folding marketing into revenue and growth roles, and fewer marketing leaders report to the CEO, now at 52%. Roles like Chief Growth Officer and Chief Commercial Officer are replacing CMOs, as companies push for direct revenue ownership. The role isn't disappearing, but being absorbed into broader functions.

articles.title: 9 tips to applying in today's market
articles.summary: Most roles are decided before they're posted, so early outreach and networking matter more than cold applications. Strong candidates show proof of work through portfolios, keep resumes clean and focused, and send short tailored pitches instead of generic intros. Researching the company, applying early, and asking thoughtful questions helps differentiate. Posting on LinkedIn also signals how candidates think and that they can build distribution.

articles.title: How to build a brand presence on Reddit in 2026
articles.summary: Reddit works best when brands learn community behavior before they try to promote anything. Start by using the free Reddit Pro tools to track keywords, analyze discussions, and monitor performance. Spend time in relevant subreddits to understand the tone and common conversations so your organic posts and ads match community expectations. Reddit has become more valuable because Google Search and AI chatbots cite its discussions more often, so a strong presence can increase product discovery and brand visibility.

articles.title: 5 Storytelling Frameworks to Attract B2B Buyers
articles.summary: A B2B narrative is not an emotional pitch but a logical thread connecting why a brand exists, what it's fighting against, and how it helps customers win. Each event in that story needs to happen because of the one before it, replacing the flat "and then" of a feature list with a "because of that" chain that creates stakes and payoff. That thread also needs an opponent, the specific practice or belief the brand positions itself against, since without one there's nothing for the story to resolve.

articles.title: Two months of silence → A booked call
articles.summary: A follow-up that converted a ghosted lead used a simple system. Claude pulls full context from Gmail and Google Drive. Claap transcripts surface exact call details like pain points and goals. Semrush adds relevant keyword and ranking insights. A quick manual check updates anything that's changed. Claude then drafts the email in the sender's voice, followed by edits.

articles.title: Video Editing With Claude: 5 AI Skills You Need to Try (34 minute video)
articles.summary: This video shows how to use Claude to cut videos with transcript-based edits, add auto-zooms, organize assets with a Slack bot, apply AI color grading, and find B-roll from across the web. The workflow uses Claude, GitHub skills, MCPs, and tools like Tela to automate repetitive editing tasks.

articles.title: If Your Brand Isn't Showing Up in YouTube AI Search, Your Competitors Are
articles.summary: Google's AI search is pulling YouTube videos into answers, often favoring creators over brands. Content that's clearly structured and built around real questions performs best. Depth across related topics also matters more than single keywords. Creators are winning by doing this well, leaving brands that rely on product-led content at risk of being left out of the answer.

articles.title: Funny Marketing 101
articles.summary: Effective marketing humor comes down to two rules that don't apply to comedy in general, being specific enough that only a narrow audience gets the joke, and being surprising enough to subvert what that audience already expects. Niche recognition works because most brands never bother creating humor built for one audience's daily frustrations, which is why an internal joke about IT teams or LinkedIn cringe can outperform anything broader. The same subversion shows up in a simple format, one where a familiar structure gets a new idea poured into it, then twisted in a way the audience doesn't see coming.

articles.title: How Poppi Built a Billion-Dollar Soda Brand
articles.summary: Poppi created its own category, then spent roughly 20% of its marketing budget on TikTok creators, making creator content the primary driver of awareness.

articles.title: Substack's new tool tells you who's been writing their newsletters with AI
articles.summary: Substack is integrating AI detection software from Pangram so readers can see an estimated human-versus-AI split for any newsletter post, note, or comment over 100 characters.

articles.title: 5 ways to use Google Search Console for keyword research
articles.summary: GSC can help you spot opportunities like striking distance keywords sitting on page 2 and low CTR pages with great rankings.
